title: Stability and quasi-normal ringing in analogue black-white holes in SNAIL-based traveling-wave parametric amplifiers
authors: Daisuke Yamauchi, Haruna Katayama, Norihiro Tanahashi
abstract: The circuit dynamics constructed by traveling-wave parametric amplifiers (TWPA), using superconducting nonlinear asymmetric elements (SNAILs), are known to be approximately described by the Korteweg-de Vries (KdV) or modified KdV equations in the continuum limit and admit soliton solutions. The soliton spatially modulates the effective propagation velocity of the weak probe field, which leads to the effective realization of the causal structure of the analogue event horizons in the SNAIL-TWPA circuit system. In this paper, we derive the master equation for the weak probe field where the background soliton acts as an effective potential. We show the absence of normalizable negative modes in the SNAIL-TWPA circuit system by using the language of supersymmetric quantum mechanics. We also present the first study of quasi-normal modes (QNM) of the SNAIL-TWPA analogue black-white hole system by semi-analytic and numerical methods. Based on the resultant QNM frequency, we clarify the timescale at which nonlinear dispersion becomes effective in the SNAIL-TWPA circuit system and demonstrate how ringdown is excited.
summary: This paper explores the stability and quasi-normal ringing of analogue black-white holes implemented in SNAIL-based traveling-wave parametric amplifiers. By mapping the circuit dynamics to KdV and mKdV equations, the authors show the system is stable and provide a scaling law for the frequency of the characteristic ringdown signal.
main_problem: The study investigates the perturbative stability and the quasi-normal mode (QNM) frequencies of analogue black-white hole systems in a superconducting circuit platform.
main_result: The authors prove the system is perturbatively stable using supersymmetric quantum mechanics and find that the fundamental QNM frequency scales with the physical parameter beta_phys approximately as |Omega| proportional to beta_phys^0.7.
method: The study employs the reductive perturbation method to derive KdV/mKdV equations, uses supersymmetric quantum mechanics for stability analysis, and applies WKB approximations and the shooting method for QNM frequency calculation.
model_or_system: The system is a traveling-wave parametric amplifier (TWPA) based on SNAIL superconducting nonlinear elements, where soliton solutions create an effective causal structure analogous to event horizons.
key_observables: Quasi-normal mode (QNM) frequencies (Omega), stability (absence of negative modes), and the timescale of nonlinear dispersion.
important_parameters: The normalized relative velocity (beta_phys), nonlinear coefficients (c3, c4), and the soliton half-width (w_phys).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a continuum limit, neglects higher-derivative nonlinear dispersion terms in certain regimes, and assumes the soliton amplitude is small enough for the reductive perturbation method to hold.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the SNAIL element and TWPA setup; Figure 2 compares different soliton shapes; Figure 3 displays effective potentials and probe field velocity; Figure 4 compares WKB orders against numerical results; Figure 5 shows WKB correction terms; Figure 6 shows the distribution of QNM roots in the complex plane.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SNAIL-TWPA system, derives the nonlinear wave equations and effective potentials, performs stability analysis via SUSY quantum mechanics, calculates QNM frequencies using WKB and numerical shooting methods, and discusses the validity of the approximations.
why_interesting: This paper is interesting for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems because it demonstrates how superconducting circuit architectures can be used as an analogue platform to study complex gravitational phenomena like event horizons and ringdown.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Schematic representation of a superconducting nonlinear asymmetric inductive element (SNAIL). It con- sists of a superconducting loop with asymmetric Joseph- son junctions: one smaller junction with Josephson energy αEJ(α < 1) and multiple larger junctions with energy EJ. An external magnetic flux threading the loop tunes the non- linearity of the effective inductance. In circuit schematics, the SNAIL is represented by a spiral symbol. (b) Schematic of a SNAIL-based traveling-wave parametric amplifier (TWPA), consisting of identical SNAIL unit cells connected in series and shunted by capacitances Cg. Here, CJ represents the ef- fective capacitance of a SNAIL, and a represents...
FIG. 2: Soliton solutions as a function of η: (a) KdV soliton, (b) mKdV+ soliton, (c) mKdV−soliton.

FIG. 3: Effective potential V (red solid) and its supersymmetric partner potential eV (blue dashed) as functions of the tortoise coordinate η∗for KdV (βphys = 0.3) [left panel], mKdV+ (βphys = 0.3) [center panel], and mKdV−(βphys = −0.3) [right panel] models. We also plot the probe field velocity v.

FIG. 4: Quasi-normal mode spectra for analogue black-white holes constructed by SNAIL-TWPA circuit system with various WKB orders; the first order [red +], the third order [green □], and the sixth order with the 3/3 Pad´e approximation [blue △]. For comparison, the quasi-normal mode spectra evaluated by the shooting method are shown in black ⃝.

FIG. 5: Second [red], third [green], fourth [cyan], fifth [blue], and sixth [magenta] order correction terms of the WKB approximation for KdV [left panel], mKdV+ [center panel], and mKdV−[right panel] models.
